facts.text: Kyoto was founded as Heian-kyō in 794, modeled after Chang'an, becoming Japan's imperial capital for over a millennium.
The city’s name means “tranquility and peace capital,” reflecting its role as a serene seat of power during the Heian period.
Kyoto’s oldest restaurant, Honke Owariya, opened in 1465 and still serves traditional dishes to this day.
In 1994, seventeen historic monuments in Kyoto were inscribed as UNESCO World Heritage Sites, preserving its ancient streets and temples.
The Tōzai subway line links Kyoto’s city center to the Keihan and Keifuku railways, a key transit link that dates back to the early 20th century.
Kyoto’s traditional matcha culture thrives in tea houses that have been serving powdered green tea for centuries, a practice highlighted by local tourism guides.
